title: Uptime Monitor
description: We build a monitor that probes real URLs every few seconds, tracks incidents, alerts once per outage, and publishes a status page to <0>./out/status.html</0>. Its uptime tallies are durable — stop the process and start it again, and the history keeps accumulating.
run_intro: Start the monitor from the app directory:
run_watch_note: <0>npm start</0> runs <1>creact --watch index.tsx</1>. Plain <2>creact index.tsx</2> converges once and exits, while <3>--watch</3> keeps the process alive so the sweep timers keep firing.
run_output_intro: After the first sweep the console shows every probe, the first alert, the rollup, and the page write:
code_run_output:
[probe] Example: up in 180ms
[probe] Retired API: down in 2ms
[probe] Wikipedia: up in 240ms
[alert] 2026-07-12T10:00:00.000Z ALERT Retired API is down (https://localhost:1)
[monitor] degraded — down: Retired API
[status-page] wrote ./out/status.html
run_output_note: The status page lands in <0>./out/status.html</0> and each alert appends one line to <1>./out/alerts.log</1>. Both are rewritten from live reactive state as sweeps come in.
entry_intro: The entry point exports a default async function that the <0>creact</0> CLI calls. It hands <1>render()</1> a root component, a file-backed ledger, and a stack name:
entry_note: <0>FileMemory</0> persists deployment state under <1>./.state</1>. That ledger is what lets the uptime tallies survive restarts later in this walkthrough.
targets_intro: Three targets define what the monitor watches, and two constants pace the sweeps:
targets_note: The Retired API points at <0>https://localhost:1</0>, a port that always refuses connections. Every run exercises the failure path, so you can watch an incident open without breaking anything real.
sweep_state_intro: The App component owns two collection signals that every check reports into:
sweep_state_note: <0>batch</0> groups the sample write and the timestamp write into one update, so downstream effects run once per probe. The <1>slice(-60)</1> keeps a rolling window of samples per target.
probe_intro: A probe is one real <0>fetch</0> with a timeout, folded into a plain sample record:
probe_note: Timeouts and refused connections land in the <0>catch</0> branch, so every probe resolves to a sample. A bad network moment never throws out of the monitor.
sweep_intro: Each <0>HttpCheck</0> keeps its latest sample in a signal and reports every result up to the App:
sweep_note: <0>latest</0> feeds this component's own effects while <1>onSample</1> feeds the shared collections. One probe, two audiences.
sweep_lifecycle_intro: The sweep loop is real timer wiring, established once when the component mounts:
sweep_lifecycle_note: <0>onMount</0> fires the first probe immediately and starts the interval. <1>onCleanup</1> registers the teardown, so disposing the tree stops the timers.
tally_intro: Uptime percentages need history that outlives the process, so each check owns a durable resource whose outputs are cumulative counters:
tally_note: The props getter is reactive, so the handler re-runs for every new sample and <0>setOutputs(prev => ...)</0> increments the persisted counters. The effect below it forwards the reactive accessors up as plain numbers.
transitions_intro: The check also announces status flips, using explicit dependency tracking:
transitions_note: <0>on</0> with <1>defer: true</1> skips the first run and hands the callback both the next and previous status. <2>untrack</2> reads the latency without subscribing to it.
checkpoint: Checkpoint: stop the process, keep <0>./.state</0>, and run <1>npm start</1> again. The handlers re-run, <2>prev</2> arrives with the persisted counters, and the uptime percentages continue from where they left off instead of resetting to 100. Delete <3>./.state</3> to start the history from scratch.
derived_intro: Back in the App, two memos derive the current picture from the raw samples:
derived_note: <0>latestSamples</0> pairs each target with its newest sample, and <1>overall</1> rolls those statuses up to up, degraded, or down. Memos cache their value, so every downstream reader shares one computation.
incidents_intro: Incidents are state rather than a view, because alerting needs to know when each outage started:
incidents_note: <0>createComputed</0> runs before effects, so the incident list is settled before anything renders or alerts from it. Returning <1>prev</1> when nothing changed keeps downstream work from re-firing.
layout_intro: The layout wires everything together with control-flow components:
layout_note: <0>For</0> mounts one <1>HttpCheck</1> per target, <2>ErrorBoundary</2> catches a check that throws, <3>Show</3> holds the page and banner back until the first sweep lands, and <4>Switch</4> picks exactly one banner for the current rollup.
banner_intro: The banner is a side-effect-only component that logs the rollup whenever it changes:
fault_intro: Its sibling handles the boundary's fallback the same way:
fault_note: Neither component renders anything. In CReact a component can exist purely for its effect, and both re-run their logging when the signals they read change.
rows_intro: The StatusPage component reduces raw samples and tallies to one row per target:
rows_note: One memo does all the shaping — current status, last probe latency, and the uptime percentage computed from the durable counters.
write_effect_intro: One effect publishes the page whenever any row changes:
write_effect_note: <0>catchError</0> boxes the render step, so a bad template logs an error instead of killing the effect. When the render succeeds, the write fires without being awaited.
render_html_intro: Rendering is plain string templating over the rows:
publish_intro: Publishing is a real filesystem write:
publish_note: Every change to the reactive state ends as bytes in <0>./out/status.html</0>. Open it in a browser and refresh as sweeps land.
alert_intro: Alerts should fire once per incident, not once per sweep. <0>createReaction</0> gives exactly that shape:
alert_note: The reaction fires once per arming, records any incident it has not seen, then re-arms itself for the next change. Each incident id includes <0>openedAt</0>, so a target that recovers and fails again alerts fresh.
alert_log_intro: Recording an alert writes to the console and appends to a log file:
alert_log_note: One line per incident lands in <0>./out/alerts.log</0>. Incident tracking is in-memory, so a restart re-opens incidents for targets that are still down and logs a fresh line.
recap_signals: Signals collect probe samples, and <0>batch</0> groups related writes into one update.
recap_lifecycle: <0>onMount</0> and <1>onCleanup</1> own the sweep timers, so disposing the tree stops them.
recap_durable: <0>useAsyncOutput</0> with a reactive props getter turns each sample into a persisted counter increment.
recap_memos: <0>createMemo</0> derives rows and rollups, and <1>createComputed</1> settles incident state before effects run.
recap_flow: <0>For</0>, <1>Show</1>, <2>Switch</2>, and <3>ErrorBoundary</3> shape the tree from reactive state.
recap_reaction: <0>createReaction</0> fires once per arming, which is exactly the alert-once contract.
challenge: Challenge: add a URL you care about to the targets list while <0>npm start</0> is running. Watch mode restarts the app, the ledger opens a fresh tally, and <1>./out/status.html</1> gains a row on the next sweep.
